title: Garlic and lemon infusion for colds
subtitle: A pungent warming brew for the first days of a cold
excerpt: Fresh garlic cloves simmered in water with lemon juice, taken at the onset of a cold. The heating drives off most of the raw-garlic harshness while preserving the antimicrobial allicin derivatives. One of the simplest and most universally available cold-season remedies.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), garlic entry, antimicrobial, expectorant, and decongestant properties. King's American Dispensatory (Felter and Lloyd, 1898), garlic decoction for colds and chest complaints. WHO Monograph on Allium sativum (1999), antimicrobial mechanisms documented.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make fresh for each cup, the allicin in cooked garlic begins to degrade within a few hours.

glossaryTerms.definition: The primary antimicrobial compound released when garlic is crushed. Heat modifies allicin into related sulphur compounds, some with retained antimicrobial activity, while reducing the harsh raw flavour.

content.text: Three cloves of crushed garlic simmered in water for ten minutes, strained, and drunk hot with lemon. The heat drives off enough of the raw garlic sharpness to make the brew palatable while retaining the antimicrobial sulphur compounds, the heated

derivatives are less volatile than raw allicin but still documented as antimicrobial. Maud Grieve lists garlic as one of the foremost cold-season remedies; the WHO monograph (1999) confirms the antimicrobial mechanism. This preparation is the quickest garlic remedy, no overnight maceration, no tincture-making, just a cup of garlic broth ready in 20 minutes.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Anticoagulants: garlic has mild antiplatelet activity. At one to three cups a day, this is unlikely to be significant for most people. If you are on warfarin, aspirin, or other anticoagulants, discuss regular therapeutic garlic use with your prescriber.

Pregnancy: culinary garlic use is well established as safe. One to two therapeutic cups a day during a cold is within a conservative culinary range. Regular therapeutic garlic infusions throughout pregnancy are best discussed with a midwife.

See your doctor for a cold that worsens after the first three days, high fever (above 38.5°C) persisting more than 48 hours, or difficulty breathing.

content.text: Crush or roughly chop {{garlic}}. Place in the small saucepan with {{water}}. Bring to a gentle simmer and simmer covered for 10 minutes. Strain through a sieve into a mug. Allow to cool slightly. Stir in honey and lemon juice if using. Drink slowly while still hot.

One cup, up to three times daily during the acute phase of a cold. Make fresh for each dose. The traditional course is three to five days, the duration of the acute cold phase. If the cold is not improving after five days, see your doctor.
When NOT to use this remedy

Discuss with prescriber if on anticoagulants. See your doctor for a cold that worsens after initial improvement, for fever above 38.5°C persisting more than 48 hours, for breathlessness, or for green or bloody mucus, these suggest bacterial secondary infection.
